docs.title: chromadose
docs.text:
Modern multichannel radiochromic film dosimetry in Python.

chromadose implements all major multichannel film dosimetry methods in one library, with built-in uncertainty estimation, gamma analysis, and PDF reporting.

docs.title: Why chromadose?
docs.text: Feature chromadose FilmQA Pro OMG Dosimetry Micke method Yes Yes No Mayer method Yes No Yes Multigaussian Yes No No Neural network Yes No No Uncertainty Every method Limited No Open source MIT Commercial GPL Vectorized Yes N/A Row-by-row

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import chromadose as cd
from chromadose.methods import MickeSolver

# Load calibration and film
cal = cd.Calibration.load("calibration.json")
film = cd.FilmScan(red=red, green=green, blue=blue)

# Convert to dose
dose_map = MickeSolver().solve(film, cal.result)

# Gamma analysis
from chromadose.analysis import gamma_2d
gamma = gamma_2d(reference, dose_map.dose, dose_criteria=3, distance_criteria_mm=3)
print(f"Pass rate: {gamma.pass_rate * 100:.1f}%")

docs.title: Methods at a Glance
docs.text: Method Speed (550x500) Reference Mayer 0.04s Mayer et al., Med. Phys. 2012 Micke 0.14s Micke, Lewis, Yu, Med. Phys. 2011 Multigaussian 4.7s Mendez et al., Phys. Med. Biol. 2018 ANN ~1s Chang et al., Phys. Med. Biol. 2025

docs.title: FilmScan
docs.text:
Scanned film with RGB channels normalized to [0, 1].

from chromadose.core.types import FilmScan

film = FilmScan(red=red_array, green=green_array, blue=blue_array, dpi=72.0)
film.shape        # (H, W)
film.rgb          # (H, W, 3)
film.pixel_size_mm  # 25.4 / dpi

docs.title: DoseMap
docs.text:
Result of film-to-dose conversion.

dose_map.dose          # (H, W) dose in Gy
dose_map.uncertainty   # (H, W) per-pixel uncertainty
dose_map.dose_r        # (H, W) red channel dose
dose_map.dose_g        # (H, W) green channel dose
dose_map.dose_b        # (H, W) blue channel dose
dose_map.method        # str
dose_map.metadata      # dict

docs.title: Calibration
docs.text:
Facade for building and managing calibrations.

import chromadose as cd

cal = cd.Calibration.from_arrays(doses, red_pixels, green_pixels, blue_pixels)
cal.save("cal.json")
cal = cd.Calibration.load("cal.json")
cal.result    # CalibrationResult with .red, .green, .blue FitParams
cal.summary() # Human-readable summary string
cal.plot_curves()  # matplotlib figure

docs.title: FitParams
docs.text:
Rational function parameters for one channel: pixel(D) = (r + sD) / (t + D)

params.pixel(dose_array)       # predict pixel values
params.dose(pixel_array)       # invert to dose
params.dpixel_ddose(dose_array)  # derivative

docs.title: MickeSolver
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MickeSolver
dose_map = MickeSolver().solve(film, calibration_result)

docs.title: MayerSolver
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MayerSolver
dose_map = MayerSolver().solve(film, calibration_result)

docs.title: MultigaussianSolver
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MultigaussianCalibration, MultigaussianSolver

mg_cal = MultigaussianCalibration(doses, pixel_samples)
solver = MultigaussianSolver(mg_cal)
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration_result)
dose_map = solver.solve_6channel(film, pre_film, calibration_result)

docs.title: ANNSolver
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import ANNCalibration, ANNSolver

ann_cal = ANNCalibration(n_hidden=32, n_ensemble=5)
ann_cal.fit(pixels, doses)
solver = ANNSolver(ann_cal)
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration_result)

docs.title: gamma_2d()
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import gamma_2d
result = gamma_2d(reference, evaluated, dose_criteria=3.0, distance_criteria_mm=3.0)
result.pass_rate       # float [0, 1]
result.gamma_map       # (H, W) array

docs.title: Profile Functions
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import extract_row_profile, compare_profiles
profile = extract_row_profile(dose, row=100, pixel_size_mm=1.0)
comp = compare_profiles(ref_profile, eval_profile)

docs.title: Registration
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import register_auto, register_manual
result = register_auto(reference, evaluated, pixel_size_mm=1.0)
result.registered  # aligned dose map

docs.title: DICOM Import
docs.text:
from chromadose.io.dicom import load_dicom_dose, resample_to_film
rt_dose = load_dicom_dose("rtdose.dcm")
resampled = resample_to_film(rt_dose, film_shape=(500, 500), film_pixel_size_mm=0.353)

docs.title: PDF Reports
docs.text:
from chromadose.io import generate_report
generate_report("report.pdf", dose_map, gamma_result=gamma, profiles=[comp])

docs.title: Image Loading
docs.text:
from chromadose.core.image import load_tiff, load_tiff_averaged
film = load_tiff("scan.tif")
film = load_tiff_averaged(["scan1.tif", "scan2.tif", "scan3.tif"])

docs.title: Command Line Interface
docs.text: chromadose provides a CLI for the full dosimetry pipeline.

docs.title: chromadose calibrate
docs.text:
Build calibration from scanned film strips.

chromadose calibrate \
  --films cal_0Gy.tif cal_1Gy.tif cal_2Gy.tif cal_4Gy.tif \
  --doses 0 1 2 4 \
  -o calibration.json

docs.title: chromadose solve
docs.text:
Convert a treatment film to a dose map.

chromadose solve \
  --film treatment.tif \
  --cal calibration.json \
  --method micke \
  -o dose.npy

Options:

  • --method: micke (default), mayer, or multigaussian
  • --plot: display the dose map

docs.title: chromadose gamma
docs.text:
Run gamma analysis between measured and reference dose.

chromadose gamma \
  --measured dose.npy \
  --reference tps_dose.npy \
  --criteria 3/3 \
  --threshold 10 \
  --pixel-size 0.353 \
  -o gamma.npy

docs.title: chromadose report
docs.text:
Generate a PDF QA report.

chromadose report \
  --measured dose.npy \
  --reference tps_dose.npy \
  --title "IMRT QA Report" \
  --patient "PATIENT001" \
  --plan "Head & Neck VMAT" \
  -o report.pdf

docs.title: Gamma Analysis
docs.text:
Reference

Low DA, Harms WB, Mutic S, Purdy JA. "A technique for the quantitative evaluation of dose distributions." Med. Phys. 1998;25(5):656-61.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
The gamma index at each reference point is:

\[\gamma(\mathbf{r}_r) = \min_{\mathbf{r}_e} \sqrt{ \frac{|\mathbf{r}_r - \mathbf{r}_e|^2}{\Delta d_M^2} + \frac{(D_r - D_e)^2}{\Delta D^2} }\]

Where \(\Delta d_M\) is the distance-to-agreement criterion (mm) and \(\Delta D\) is the dose-difference criterion.

Points with \(\gamma \leq 1\) pass the criteria.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import gamma_2d

result = gamma_2d(
    reference=tps_dose,
    evaluated=film_dose,
    dose_criteria=3.0,           # 3%
    distance_criteria_mm=3.0,    # 3mm
    pixel_size_mm=0.353,         # e.g., 72 DPI film
    dose_threshold_pct=10.0,     # exclude < 10% of max
    dose_criteria_is_global=True,
)

print(f"Pass rate: {result.pass_rate * 100:.1f}%")
print(f"Points: {result.points_passed}/{result.points_evaluated}")

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Default Description dose_criteria 3.0 Dose difference criterion (%) distance_criteria_mm 3.0 DTA criterion (mm) pixel_size_mm 1.0 Pixel size in mm dose_threshold_pct 10.0 Exclude below this % of max dose_criteria_is_global True Global (max dose) or local (per-point) max_gamma 2.0 Cap gamma values

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
GammaResult contains:

  • gamma_map — 2D gamma index array (NaN below threshold)
  • pass_rate — fraction of points with \(\gamma \leq 1\)
  • criteria — human-readable string, e.g. "3%/3mm global"
  • points_evaluated, points_passed — counts

docs.title: Dose Profiles
docs.text: Extract and compare 1D dose profiles from 2D dose maps.

docs.title: Extraction
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import extract_row_profile, extract_col_profile, extract_line_profile

# Horizontal profile
profile = extract_row_profile(dose_map.dose, row=100, pixel_size_mm=0.353)

# Vertical profile
profile = extract_col_profile(dose_map.dose, col=150, pixel_size_mm=0.353)

# Arbitrary line
profile = extract_line_profile(
    dose_map.dose,
    start=(50, 20), end=(150, 180),  # (row, col) pixel coords
    n_points=200,
    pixel_size_mm=0.353,
)

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import compare_profiles

comp = compare_profiles(reference=tps_profile, evaluated=film_profile)

print(f"Mean abs diff: {comp.mean_abs_diff_pct:.1f}%")
print(f"Max abs diff: {comp.max_abs_diff_pct:.1f}%")

ProfileComparison contains:

  • dose_diff — absolute dose difference at each point
  • dose_diff_pct — difference as % of reference max
  • mean_abs_diff_pct, max_abs_diff_pct — summary metrics

docs.title: Film-to-TPS Registration
docs.text: Spatially align a film measurement to a TPS dose distribution before gamma analysis.

docs.title: Automatic Registration
docs.text:
Optimizes translation and rotation to minimize dose differences:

from chromadose.analysis import register_auto

result = register_auto(
    reference=tps_dose,
    evaluated=film_dose,
    pixel_size_mm=0.353,
    max_shift_mm=10.0,
    max_angle_deg=5.0,
)

print(f"Shift: dx={result.dx_mm:.1f}mm, dy={result.dy_mm:.1f}mm")
print(f"Rotation: {result.angle_deg:.2f} degrees")

# Use the registered image for gamma analysis
gamma = gamma_2d(tps_dose, result.registered, ...)

docs.title: Manual Registration
docs.text:
Apply a known shift and rotation:

from chromadose.analysis import register_manual

result = register_manual(
    film_dose,
    dx_mm=2.5,
    dy_mm=-1.0,
    angle_deg=0.5,
    pixel_size_mm=0.353,
)

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Uses scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates for bilinear interpolation
  2. L-BFGS-B optimization of translation (x, y) and rotation (angle)
  3. Cost function: mean squared dose difference in the above-threshold region

docs.title: Basic Install
docs.text: pip install chromadose

docs.title: Optional Extras
docs.text:
# DICOM RT Dose import (requires pydicom)
pip install chromadose[dicom]

# PyTorch GPU acceleration for neural network method
pip install chromadose[ann-gpu]

# Development tools (pytest, mypy, ruff)
pip install chromadose[dev]

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/cptfinch/chromadose.git
cd chromadose
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.11
  • numpy >= 1.24
  • scipy >= 1.10
  • tifffile >= 2023.1
  • matplotlib >= 3.7

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
import chromadose
print(chromadose.__version__)  # 1.0.0

Or from the command line:

chromadose --version

docs.title: 1. Build Calibration
docs.text:
Scan your calibration film strips and extract mean pixel values at each known dose:

import chromadose as cd

cal = cd.Calibration.from_arrays(
    doses=[0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 7, 9],       # Gy
    red_pixels=[0.221, 0.205, 0.190, ...],  # mean red at each dose
    green_pixels=[0.042, 0.041, 0.040, ...],
    blue_pixels=[0.067, 0.065, 0.063, ...],
)

# Save for reuse
cal.save("calibration.json")

Or load TIFF files directly:

from chromadose.core.image import load_tiff

film = load_tiff("calibration_strip.tif")

docs.title: 2. Convert Film to Dose
docs.text:
from chromadose.core.image import load_tiff
from chromadose.methods import MickeSolver

cal = cd.Calibration.load("calibration.json")
film = load_tiff("treatment_film.tif")

solver = MickeSolver()
dose_map = solver.solve(film, cal.result)

print(f"Max dose: {dose_map.dose.max():.2f} Gy")
print(f"Uncertainty: {dose_map.uncertainty.mean():.3f} Gy (mean)")

docs.title: 3. Try Different Methods
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MayerSolver

mayer_result = MayerSolver().solve(film, cal.result)

docs.title: 4. Gamma Analysis
docs.text:
from chromadose.analysis import gamma_2d

gamma = gamma_2d(
    reference=tps_dose,          # from TPS (DICOM or npy)
    evaluated=dose_map.dose,     # from film
    dose_criteria=3.0,           # 3%
    distance_criteria_mm=3.0,    # 3mm
    pixel_size_mm=film.pixel_size_mm,
)

print(f"Pass rate: {gamma.pass_rate * 100:.1f}%")

docs.title: 5. Generate Report
docs.text:
from chromadose.io import generate_report

generate_report(
    "qa_report.pdf",
    dose_map,
    gamma_result=gamma,
    reference_dose=tps_dose,
    title="IMRT QA Report",
    patient_id="PATIENT001",
)

docs.title: 6. Command Line
docs.text:
chromadose calibrate --films cal*.tif --doses 0 0.5 1 2 4 7 9 -o cal.json
chromadose solve --film treatment.tif --cal cal.json --method micke -o dose.npy
chromadose gamma --measured dose.npy --reference tps.npy --criteria 3/3
chromadose report --measured dose.npy --reference tps.npy -o report.pdf

docs.title: Neural Network Method (ANN)
docs.text:
Reference

Chang L-Y, et al. "GANN: a generalized artificial neural network for multichannel radiochromic film dosimetry." Phys. Med. Biol. 2025.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
A feedforward neural network that learns the nonlinear mapping from RGB pixel values to dose directly from calibration data. Key advantages:

  • No rational function assumption
  • Can potentially generalize across film batches
  • Uncertainty from ensemble of networks

Architecture:

Input: [R, G, B] (3 neurons)
  → Hidden 1: 32 neurons, ReLU
  → Hidden 2: 32 neurons, ReLU
  → Output: dose (1 neuron, ReLU)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import ANNCalibration, ANNSolver

# Train on calibration data
ann_cal = ANNCalibration(n_hidden=32, n_ensemble=5)
ann_cal.fit(pixels, doses)  # (N, 3), (N,)

# Solve
solver = ANNSolver(ann_cal)
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration)

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Pure numpy/scipy — no PyTorch dependency for the core implementation
  • L-BFGS-B optimization with He initialization
  • Bootstrap sampling for ensemble diversity
  • Uncertainty from standard deviation across ensemble members

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Training: depends on data size and max_iter
  • Inference on 550x500: ~1s (forward pass through ensemble)

docs.title: Mayer Method (2012)
docs.text:
Reference

Mayer RR, et al. "An improved dose response function for multichannel film dosimetry." Med. Phys. 2012;39(12):7596-602.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
An analytical solution that avoids iterative optimization. Uses the derivatives of the calibration curves to compute an optimally-weighted dose from the per-channel estimates.

Equation 6 (optimized dose):

\[D_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\sum_c w_c D_c}{\sum_c w_c}\]

where \(w_c = \left(\frac{dD_c}{d\text{pixel}_c}\right)^{-2}\) is the inverse-variance weight from equation 7.

The method also provides a disturbance map (equation 2) and residual error (equation 9) for quality metrics.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MayerSolver

solver = MayerSolver()
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration)

# Mayer-specific metadata
disturbance = dose_map.metadata["disturbance"]
residual = dose_map.metadata["residual_error"]

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• 550x500 image: 0.04s (fastest method)
  • Fully analytical — no iterations needed

docs.title: Micke Method (2011)
docs.text:
Reference

Micke A, Lewis DF, Yu X. "Multichannel film dosimetry with nonuniformity correction." Med. Phys. 2011;38(5):2523-34.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
The original multichannel method. For each pixel, find the dose \(D\) that minimizes the sum of squared residuals across all color channels:

\[D^* = \arg\min_D \sum_{c \in \{R,G,B\}} \left[ \text{pixel}_c - \frac{r_c + s_c D}{t_c + D} \right]^2\]

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: chromadose uses vectorized Gauss-Newton refinement — not per-pixel scipy.optimize. This processes the entire image in ~10 Newton iterations, fully vectorized over all pixels simultaneously.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MickeSolver

solver = MickeSolver()
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration)

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• 550x500 image: 0.14s
  • Uncertainty: from inter-channel disagreement (std of per-channel doses)

docs.title: Multigaussian Method (2018)
docs.text:
First Open-Source Implementation

chromadose provides the first open-source implementation of the Multigaussian method.

Reference

Mendez I, Polsak A, Hudej R, Casar B. "The Multigaussian method: a new approach to mitigating spatial heterogeneities with multichannel radiochromic film dosimetry." Phys. Med. Biol. 2018;63(17):175013.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
Models the full joint probability distribution of pixel values as a multivariate Gaussian at each dose:

\[p(\mathbf{x} | D) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\mu}(D), \boldsymbol{\Sigma}(D))\]

Dose estimation by maximum likelihood:

\[D^* = \arg\min_D \left[ (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}(D))^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}(D)^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}(D)) + \ln|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(D)| \right]\]

Key advantages:

  • Uses the full covariance structure between channels
  • Naturally handles inter-channel correlations
  • Provides principled uncertainty from the probabilistic model
  • Demonstrated 0.8% mean absolute error (best published result)

docs.title: 3-Channel Mode
docs.text:
from chromadose.methods import MultigaussianCalibration, MultigaussianSolver

# Build calibration from ROI pixel samples
mg_cal = MultigaussianCalibration(doses=doses, pixel_samples=samples)

solver = MultigaussianSolver(mg_cal)
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration)

docs.title: 6-Channel Mode (Pre-Irradiation)
docs.text:
When pre-irradiation scans are available, the response vector becomes [R_pre, G_pre, B_pre, R_post, G_post, B_post]:

mg_cal = MultigaussianCalibration.from_film_rois(
    doses=doses, films=post_films, rois=rois, pre_films=pre_films,
)

solver = MultigaussianSolver(mg_cal)
dose_map = solver.solve_6channel(film, pre_film, calibration)

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• 550x500 image: 4.7s (optimized from 133s via vectorized grid search + parabolic interpolation)
  • Dense 200-point grid evaluation, all pixels processed simultaneously
  • Uncertainty computed from NLL curvature at zero extra cost

docs.title: Dosimetry Methods
docs.text: chromadose implements four multichannel film dosimetry methods. All share the same calibration model — the rational function \(\text{pixel}(D) = \frac{r + sD}{t + D}\) — but differ in how they combine information from multiple color channels to estimate dose.

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text:
Method Year Approach Speed Best for Micke 2011 Least-squares multichannel 0.14s General use, original method Mayer 2012 Analytical optimization 0.04s Speed-critical applications Multigaussian 2018 Maximum likelihood (MLE) 4.7s Best accuracy, research ANN 2025 Neural network ~1s Batch-independent calibration

Times measured on a 550x500 pixel film image.

docs.title: Unified API
docs.text:
All methods share the same interface:

solver = SomeMethod()
dose_map = solver.solve(film, calibration)

# Every method returns:
dose_map.dose          # (H, W) dose in Gy
dose_map.uncertainty   # (H, W) per-pixel uncertainty
dose_map.dose_r        # (H, W) red channel dose
dose_map.dose_g        # (H, W) green channel dose
dose_map.dose_b        # (H, W) blue channel dose
dose_map.method        # "micke", "mayer", "multigaussian", or "ann"
